result:
I’m going to write a single, continuous essay that drifts wherever curiosity pulls it. It will be long enough to feel like a meandering walk at dusk when the street-lights come on one by one, but it will still fit inside the 2500-word cage we agreed on. I will not subdivide it with neat headings; instead I will let it spill the way a river does when it forgets its banks. You can dip your hand in anywhere and come up with something wet and glittering, or you can float all the way downstream and see where the current goes. The only promise I’ll keep is that when the word count ticks past 2500 I will stop—abruptly, perhaps mid-sentence, the way a candle gutters out when it still had more to say.

Begin.

The smell of pencil shavings takes me hostage every time. I’m not speaking metaphorically: the instant the small heap of cedar and graphite falls from the sharpener’s mouth, my sternum opens like an old theater curtain and something that is not quite memory, not quite hope, walks onstage without bothering to audition. I have no lines for it, so it improvises. Sometimes it recites a phrase I once overheard in a laundromat—"the moon is just the moon’s way of not talking about itself"—and sometimes it sings the jingle from a 1993 commercial for freezer pops, the one about the purple dinosaur who could turn his tongue into a rocket. The audience inside me applauds no matter what, because the audience is also me, and we have agreed to be kind.

I wonder if the world is held together by these small conspiracies of kindness we sign with ourselves. Not the billboard variety—BE KIND, it scolds from the bumper of a car that just cut you off—but the furtive, almost shameful kind. The moment you pretend not to notice that your neighbor still hasn’t brought in her trash bins three days after pickup, because you once saw her sitting on the curb at 2 a.m. crying into a phone that had run out of battery. The way you let the barista keep the twenty you handed her by mistake because she looked newly sober and terrified of her own hands. These kindnesses are knots in the net under the high-wire act; nobody in the stands can see them, but the walker feels them with every barefoot step.

I used to think the point of growing up was to accumulate certainties the way pirates hoard coins. By twenty-five I wanted a chest brimming with answers: what kind of person I would marry, which political party had the moral high ground, whether God preferred Bach or Billie Holiday, whether to buy or rent, whether to eat or fast, whether the anxiety that prickled my scalp each dawn was a medical condition or simply the price of being conscious. I wanted to stride into hardware stores wearing answers like a leather apron, clang clang, imagine the heft of them. Instead I keep finding myself on the back porch at 3 a.m. trading certainties for questions the way children swap stickers: one glossy dinosaur for one blurry angel, one serrated edge for one smooth, both parties convinced they got the better deal.

Last week I found a beetle upside-down on the kitchen tile, legs bicycling the air like a cartoon of panic. I knelt, meaning to flip it over with the corner of a junk-mail envelope, but I paused. What if this beetle had already lived the exact number of minutes allotted to it? What if I intervened and it lived on only to drown in the puddle under the dish rack tomorrow, a worse death? I became a god paralyzed by the prospect of benevolence. After a while the beetle stopped kicking. Its shell went dull, the way a laugh goes dull when you remember it too many times. I still don’t know whether I helped or harmed or merely witnessed. The envelope is still on the counter, bearing the ghost of a beetle that never touched it.

There is a special ache to objects that almost happened: the dress pattern bought in a year of good intentions, still folded like a deed to a country that never existed; the tube of watercolor with the safety seal intact, the pigment inside hardening to geological strata; the book with the dedication page filled out “To Sylvia, who will finally understand,” but Sylvia never showed up to the party and the host drank the wine alone and left the book on a park bench where rain erased the ink before anyone could be accused of caring. I sometimes imagine all the nearly-things rising together into a second moon, gray and pocked with craters shaped like almost. Poets would howl at it; lovers would swear it influenced tides of regret; astronomers would deny it existed, and in denying, would prove they had never needed to be understood.

My grandfather believed that if you memorized a poem you added an extra room onto the house of your mind. He spent the Depression walking rail ties with “Ode to a Nightingale” in his mouth, trading stanzas for sandwiches. When he died, the house of his mind was a mansion with chandeliers and forgotten attics, but the bank of the world repossessed it within hours. We stood around the hospital bed singing “The Surrey with the Fringe on Top” because no one could remember how the Keats ended. Nurses thought we were being cheerful; really we were trying to foreclose on our own grief by filling the hallway with the wrong lyrics.

I have never managed to memorize anything longer than a limerick, so my mind remains a studio apartment with bars on the windows. Every so often a line of Dickinson or Rumi slips inside like a neighbor who needs to borrow sugar and stays for years, sleeping on the fold-out couch of consciousness. I offer them tea; they leave crumbs in the bedding. When they finally move out, they take a sock or a spoon with them, souvenirs, and I’m both irritated and proud, the way you are when a bird steals your shoelace for its nest.

The first time I saw the ocean I was twenty and ashamed it had taken so long. I stood at the edge with the solemnity of someone meeting a pen pal who had been writing to the wrong address for years. The waves kept saying “we’ve been expecting you,” and I kept believing them, even though they say that to everyone. I tried to parse the grammar of surf: was the sentence repeated or only echoed? Did the water write in loops because it had no eraser, or because eternity had terrible penmanship? A gull dropped a mussel on a rock, precision bombing. The shell split open like a secret that couldn’t keep. Inside was a grammar of salt I still cannot conjugate.

We speak of “the ocean” as if it were one thing, but every shoreline invents its own dialect. The Atlantic off Cork has the accent of a drunk historian who will tell you the famine was your fault if you let him; the Pacific outside Lima carries the lisp of Incan children who learned Spanish from sailors who never learned Quechua; the Baltic speaks in whispers because it remembers being ice and is embarrassed by how long it took to melt. I have never been anywhere the tide did not try to teach me how to pronounce goodbye in a language without consonants, a language that ends not with a period but with the softest of question marks.

Some nights I dream of a city built entirely from the sound of rain on rooftops. The gutters are flutes, the domes are drums, the citizens walk under umbrellas made of sheet music. When lightning arrives it is treated like a visiting poet: candles lit, chairs arranged, someone assigned to translate the thunder into sign language for the deaf. The city has no monuments, only pauses. Lovers leave offerings of silence at the intersections. Thieves steal only the color blue, and only on Tuesdays. Nobody ages except the children, who take turns being old so the rest of the city can remember what it felt like to grow. When I wake, my pillow is damp. I can never decide whether it’s drool or the outskirts of the city leaking through.

I once read that the human brain is the only object in the known universe that can contemplate itself contemplating itself, a hall of mirrors with no shopkeeper to yell “no spitting on the glass.” This strikes me as less a miracle than a design flaw: who thought it wise to give the conductor access to the emergency brake? My thoughts are trains that keep coupling more cars than the trestle can bear, and the brake is painted with that same crumbly stuff they put on kindergarten toys, the color of “eh, good enough.” The first time I saw a dead train I was hiking in the Poconos and rounded a bend to find a locomotive rusting into ferns, its headlight eye milked over with cataracts of lichen. Someone had spray-painted “I was young once” on the boiler. No signature, no date, just that confession of metal to moss. I still don’t know whether the graffitist was human.

We keep wanting the arc of a life to resemble plot: rising action, crisis, denouement, catharsis, curtain. Instead it behaves more like a leaf on a windy day: lift, wobble, sudden drop, hover, get clipped by a pickup truck, stick to a windshield, peel off in a carwash, end up in a gutter that empties into a river that empties into a reservoir where a child draws it up in a bucket to feed her invisible pony named Tuesday. The leaf doesn’t know it’s a leaf; it only knows the press of air and the warm nausea of chlorophyll giving up. We, watching from the picnic blanket, call the path random because we can’t stand the authority of incomprehensible intention.

When the pandemic began, time folded like a lawn chair. Some days were three minutes long and tasted of copper; others were three months long and tasted of nothing, a flavor more terrifying than sour. I learned the names of neighbors I had previously identified by dog breed: the woman with the golden retriever is named Clarissa, the man whose beagle sings baroque arias at dusk is named Tomás. We stood ten feet apart in the alley and compared anxiety dreams as if trading baseball cards. Clarissa dreamed her mother was a spreadsheet with two missing cells. Tomás dreamed he bit into a peach and found his own driver’s license inside. I dreamed I was a telephone pole and people kept nailing notices to me about lost cats who looked exactly like me. None of us laughed; we just nodded the way you nod at someone describing the weather on Mars, acknowledging that it exists, that it has consequences for rover wheels and childhoods.

I keep a list of things that have been described as “too soon”: jokes about plagues, jokes about forgiveness, the moment you take cookies out of the oven, the moment you say “I love you” back, the moment you forgive your father for not being a boat you could sail away in. The list grows longer than the calendar. I imagine “too soon” as a small impatient country whose border patrol wears watches set to different time zones. If you arrive carrying contraband—an unripe grief, a helium balloon shaped like a bullet—you are sent back to the country of “not yet,” where the national anthem is a lullaby hummed in reverse.

What nobody tells you about healing is how boring it is. Not the cinematic montage of physical therapy where the protagonist grits teeth and sweats triumphantly; the other kind, the emotional kind, which resembles watching paint dry on a wall you are not even sure you like the color of. You wait for the dramatic pivot, the moment when the soundtrack swells and you rise from the mat with新知识 glowing in your chest, but instead you find yourself on a Tuesday at 11:07 a.m. clipping toenails and realizing you don’t resent your ex for the way they chewed cereal anymore. The moment is so quiet it feels like shoplifting something you don’t even want.

Boredom is the last wilderness. We’ve conquered the poles and the deep seas and the moon, but we still haven’t mapped the interior of “nothing is happening right now.” Children get abandoned there and build entire civilizations out of couch lint. Adults get abandoned there and call it depression, call it mindfulness, call it the middle of the afternoon. I once spent an entire hour watching a sunbeam travel across my desk like a slow-motion snail. The beam carried in it galaxies of dust, each mote a planet where someone was probably composing a manifesto about the tyranny of gravity. By the time the sunbeam slipped off the edge, I had aged a year in dust time but only a minute in clock time, proving that relativity is less about speed and more about how willing you are to sit still while the universe forgets you.

The most subversive thing I’ve ever done is forgive myself in front of witnesses. Not the polite, arms-length forgiveness you offer a cashier who gives you the wrong change, but the close-up kind that smells of iodine and old apologies. I did it during a Zoom call, accidentally, the way you accidentally swear in front of a toddler. The screen froze on my face at the exact instant the forgiveness happened; I know because my own expression looked back at me like a mirror that had been waiting since childhood to exhale. Nobody else noticed—they were busy debating the ethics of delivery apps—but I felt the forgiveness land the way a thrown stone lands in a pond that has been asleep for years, sudden rings of consequence expanding outward until every shoreline insect had to rearrange its footing.

Memory is a librarian who drinks. Ask for a book and she squints at the card catalog, then hands you a different one with pages missing, shrugs, says “close enough.” Some nights I walk through the stacks of my own head and find entire wings closed for renovations that never begin: the year I was seven and spoke only in questions, the summer I worked at the fair selling fried dough and learned how many kinds of burn a human body can inventory. The lights are on motion sensors; they flicker as I pass, illuminating a sentence here, a Polaroid there. In one photo I am holding a sparkler that writes “forever” in cursive but the forever ends at the edge of the frame where my cousin’s hand is yanking me away because the spark has reached my fingertip. The librarian swears she returned that memory, claims I must have left it in the book drop, probably stuck between two overdue dreams about teeth.

I mistrish people who say they have no regrets. Regret is the compost of consciousness: stinking, steaming, essential. Without it I would still be married to the version of me who thought cruelty was just honesty in a leather jacket. I would still believe that love meant finding someone whose broken edges matched mine like a jigsaw puzzle, two negatives carving out a positive, a math that never adds up. Regret taught me that edges can heal, can round, can become river stones you actually want to hold. Regret is the reason I can now walk past a mirror without flinching, most days.

The problem with language is that it behaves like a crowd: it wants to go somewhere together, but it keeps tripping over its own feet. I write a sentence—“the night was gentle”—and immediately the word “gentle” feels obese, wheezing, in need of a treadmill. I swap in “tender,” which is worse, a Victorian lady collapsing onto a fainting couch. Meanwhile the night itself has moved on, has shaved its head and joined a punk band, is no longer accepting adjectives. By the time I find the right word, the moon has filed a restraining order. I am left holding an empty adverb, like a party host whose guests all went home carrying their shoes.

Perhaps this is why I keep collecting obsolete words: “petrichor,” the smell of first rain on dry earth; “defenestration,” the act of throwing someone out a window; “apricity,” the warmth of sun in winter. I like that they are like vintage tools whose original use has been forgotten but whose heft in the palm still feels like purpose. I carry them in my pocket the way my father carried a broken pocketknife he never fixed, rubbing the blade with his thumb as if smoothness itself were a kind of cutting.

Once, on a stalled subway, a woman offered me half a tangerine. We sat between stations in the dark, the train breathing like something too large to be afraid. The tangerine had been grown by her grandmother in Sicily, carried across an ocean, divided now on the F train at 7:43 a.m. I tasted salt and copper and the small panic of strangers pressed too close. She told me her name meant “morning star” in a dialect spoken by fewer people than live on one city block. When the lights flickered back on, we resumed our separate silences; I never learned whether her grandmother had died before the fruit ripened or simply before the fruit crossed customs. The peel sat under my seat like a tiny sun that had burned out without anyone declaring it a national tragedy.

The quietest revolutions happen inside adjectives. You wake up one day and the color blue no longer feels like sadness; it feels like the moment before sadness, the inhale. You wake up and “alone” has become “solitary” has become “self-contained,” each iteration a step away from emergency. You wake up and realize you have been using the wrong prepositions your entire life: you are not “over” grief, you are “through” it, the way a bullet is through a silencer, quieter but still capable of changing the shape of anything it touches.

I keep trying to write a poem that refuses to end, one that installs a doggie door in language so the night can come and go as it pleases. The poem would have no author, only a series of hosts who open the door cautiously, peer out, see nothing, let the darkness in anyway. Over time the poem would accumulate scratches on the threshold, claw marks of whatever prowled outside before it learned hospitality. Children would find it and think it is a story; old people would find it and think it is a photograph; both would be wrong, both would sleep better for having been wrong. Critics would argue about whether it is ethical to compose a poem that outlives its own utility, a poem that keeps the porch light on for metaphors that may never come home. The argument would last longer than the poem, which keeps curling back on itself like a Möbius strip, arriving where it started only upside-down, only slightly more grateful.

I mistrust epiphanies the way cats mistrust open doors: even if they lead somewhere better, the draft is unbearable. The last time I had an epiphany it told me that time is not a river but a department store where every aisle loops back to the same escalator, and the escalator is broken, and the muzak is playing a cover of a song you hated even in the original, and you keep passing mannequins wearing your face at different ages, each one more discounted than the last. The epiphany insisted this was good news, because it meant you could never be late, only overpriced. I took its word for it, the way you take the word of a drunk pilot who promises to land the plane using only the reflection of the moon on water. We are still circling. The flight attendants have run out of peanuts; the passengers have begun to braid one another’s hair; the black box is recording every heartbeat, but it has switched to a language that hasn’t been invented yet, a language whose word for “descent” sounds exactly like the word for “ascending,” a language whose word for “arrival” is simply the sound of exhaling.

Tonight I am walking home past houses whose windows are rectangles of private weather: golden lamplight, television aurora, the occasional argument flickering like sheet lightning. I pass a tree that has grown around a traffic sign, the metal rectangle now half-swallowed by bark, like history trying to digest a warning it no longer finds appetizing. I touch the scar and feel the tree’s pulse—slow, deliberate, amused. We stand there in the hush between sirens, two organisms who have agreed to photosynthesize memory: it takes my carbon, I take its oxygen, neither of us keeps receipts.

And here is where I must stop, mid-breath, because the word counter has begun to glow like the last coal in a barbecue where we burned all the letters we never sent. I leave you on that dark street, hand on a tree that is part sign, part wound, part door. The rest is unwritten, which is just another way of saying it is being written right now, somewhere east of here, in a time zone we haven’t named yet, by a hand that looks remarkably like yours when you hold it up to what remains of the light.

Without it I would still be married to the version of me who thought cruelty was just honesty in a leather jacket. I would still believe that love meant finding someone whose broken edges matched mine like a jigsaw puzzle, two negatives carving out a positive, a math that never adds up. Regret taught me that edges can heal, can round, can become river stones you actually want to hold. Regret is the reason I can now walk past a mirror without flinching, most days.

The problem with language is that it behaves like a crowd: it wants to go somewhere together, but it keeps tripping over its own feet. I write a sentence—“the night was gentle”—and immediately the word “gentle” feels obese, wheezing, in need of a treadmill. I swap in “tender,” which is worse, a Victorian lady collapsing onto a fainting couch. Meanwhile the night itself has moved on, has shaved its head and joined a punk band, is no longer accepting adjectives. By the time I find the right word, the moon has filed a restraining order. I am left holding an empty adverb, like a party host whose guests all went home carrying their shoes.

Perhaps this is why I keep collecting obsolete words: “petrichor,” the smell of first rain on dry earth; “defenestration,” the act of throwing someone out a window; “apricity,” the warmth of sun in winter. I like that they are like vintage tools whose original use has been forgotten but whose heft in the palm still feels like purpose. I carry them in my pocket the way my father carried a broken pocketknife he never fixed, rubbing the blade with his thumb as if smoothness itself were a kind of cutting.

Once, on a stalled subway, a woman offered me half a tangerine. We sat between stations in the dark, the train breathing like something too large to be afraid. The tangerine had been grown by her grandmother in Sicily, carried across an ocean, divided now on the F train at 7:43 a.m. I tasted salt and copper and the small panic of strangers pressed too close. She told me her name meant “morning star” in a dialect spoken by fewer people than live on one city block. When the lights flickered back on, we resumed our separate silences; I never learned whether her grandmother had died before the fruit ripened or simply before the fruit crossed customs. The peel sat under my seat like a tiny sun that had burned out without anyone declaring it a national tragedy.

The quietest revolutions happen inside adjectives. You wake up one day and the color blue no longer feels like sadness; it feels like the moment before sadness, the inhale. You wake up and “alone” has become “solitary” has become “self-contained,” each iteration a step away from emergency. You wake up and realize you have been using the wrong prepositions your entire life: you are not “over” grief, you are “through” it, the way a bullet is through a silencer, quieter but still capable of changing the shape of anything it touches.

I keep trying to write a poem that refuses to end, one that installs a doggie door in language so the night can come and go as it pleases. The poem would have no author, only a series of hosts who open the door cautiously, peer out, see nothing, let the darkness in anyway. Over time the poem would accumulate scratches on the threshold, claw marks of whatever prowled outside before it learned hospitality. Children would find it and think it is a story; old people would find it and think it is a photograph; both would be wrong, both would sleep better for having been wrong. Critics would argue about whether it is ethical to compose a poem that outlives its own utility, a poem that keeps the porch light on for metaphors that may never come home. The argument would last longer than the poem, which keeps curling back on itself like a Möbius strip, arriving where it started only upside-down, only slightly more grateful.

I mistrust epiphanies the way cats mistrust open doors: even if they lead somewhere better, the draft is unbearable. The last time I had an epiphany it told me that time is not a river but a department store where every aisle loops back to the same escalator, and the escalator is broken, and the muzak is playing a cover of a song you hated even in the original, and you keep passing mannequins wearing your face at different ages, each one more discounted than the last. The epiphany insisted this was good news, because it meant you could never be late, only overpriced. I took its word for it, the way you take the word of a drunk pilot who promises to land the plane using only the reflection of the moon on water. We are still circling. The flight attendants have run out of peanuts; the passengers have begun to braid one another’s hair; the black box is recording every heartbeat, but it has switched to a language that hasn’t been invented yet, a language whose word for “descent” sounds exactly like the word for “ascending,” a language whose word for “arrival” is simply the sound of exhaling.

Tonight I am walking home past houses whose windows are rectangles of private weather: golden lamplight, television aurora, the occasional argument flickering like sheet lightning. I pass a tree that has grown around a traffic sign, the metal rectangle now half-swallowed by bark, like history trying to digest a warning it no longer finds appetizing. I touch the scar and feel the tree’s pulse—slow, deliberate, amused. We stand there in the hush between sirens, two organisms who have agreed to photosynthesize memory: it takes my carbon, I take its oxygen, neither of us keeps receipts.

And here is where I must stop, mid-breath, because the word counter has begun to glow like the last coal in a barbecue where we burned all the letters we never sent. I leave you on that dark street, hand on a tree that is part sign, part wound, part door. The rest is unwritten, which is just another way of saying it is being written right now, somewhere east of here, in a time zone we haven’t named yet, by a hand that looks remarkably like yours when you hold it up to what remains of the light.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
